heading: Staff; office equipment and supplies
label: 274 Staff; office equipment and supplies
text: The Legislative Counsel shall, subject to the approval of the President pro tempore of the Senate, employ and fix the compensation of such Assistant Counsel, clerks, and other employees, and purchase such furniture, office equipment, books, stationery, and other supplies, as may be necessary for the proper performance of the duties of the Office and as may be appropriated for by Congress.
notes: CodificationAs originally enacted, section also provided for legislative counsel of House of Representatives, subject to approval of Speaker, to employ and fix the compensation of assistant counsel, clerks, etc. In view of nonapplicability of section to Speaker, employee, etc., of the House of Representatives pursuant to , set out as a note under , section has been revised to limit applicability to authority of Legislative Counsel of the Senate. See section 282a et seq. of this title for provisions relating to appointment of staff, etc., for Office of Legislative Counsel of the House of Representatives.section 531 of Pub. L. 91–510section 281 of this title
Amendments1941—Act , substituted “President pro tempore of the Senate” for “President of the Senate”.Sept. 20, 1941

Designation of Deputy Legislative Counsel, , , provided that:Pub. L. 106–57, title I, § 6113 Stat. 412Sept. 29, 1999“The Legislative Counsel may, subject to the approval of the President pro tempore of the Senate, designate one of the Senior Counsels appointed under section 102 of the Legislative Branch Appropriation Act, 1979 ( note; ; ) as Deputy Legislative Counsel. The Deputy Legislative Counsel shall perform the functions of the Legislative Counsel during the absence or disability of the Legislative Counsel, or when the office is vacant.”2 U.S.C. 274Public Law 95–39192 Stat. 771

Senior Counsel, , , provided in part that:Pub. L. 85–7571 Stat. 251July 1, 1957“No more than three employees in the Office of the Legislative Counsel of the Senate may be designated as Senior Counsel, whose compensation shall be $15,500 gross per annum each.”

Additional Senior Counsel, , , provided that:Pub. L. 95–391, title I, § 10292 Stat. 771Sept. 30, 1978“Effective , the number of employees in the Office of the Legislative Counsel of the Senate who may be designated as, and receive the compensation of, a Senior Counsel is increased to five.”October 1, 1978, , , provided in part:Pub. L. 88–24877 Stat. 804Dec. 30, 1963“That effective , one additional employee in the Office of the Legislative Counsel of the Senate may be designated as Senior Counsel, and the compensation of the additional employee so designated shall be equal to the gross per annum rate presently authorized for other employees so designated.”July 1, 1963

Increases in Compensation of Four Senior Counsels, , , eff. , which provided in part that the four Senior Counsels in the Office of the Legislative Counsel of the Senate shall each be paid at an annual rate of compensation of $39,000, was repealed by , , .Pub. L. 94–59, title I, § 10589 Stat. 275Pub. L. 116–94, div. E, title II, § 212(a)(3)(A)133 Stat. 2775July 25, 1975July 1, 1975Dec. 20, 2019, , , eff. , provided in part that the four Senior Counsels in the Office of the Legislative Counsel of the Senate shall each be paid at an annual rate of compensation of $37,620.Pub. L. 93–371, § 488 Stat. 429Aug. 13, 1974July 1, 1974

Increases in CompensationIncreases in compensation for officers and employees of the Senate under authority of the Federal Pay Comparability Act of 1970 (), see Salary Directives of the President pro tempore of the Senate, set out as notes under .Pub. L. 91–656section 4571 of this title
